The Colcloughs of Tintern Abbey, Co.
Wexford, were very popular, and real sport
ing patrons of the game, ‘They owned con-
rable property in the “Black County,”
ituated at the Wexford side of the base of
_ the Blackstair Mountains around Kiltealy,
known as the Duffry. Dufiry Hall, one
~ of the family residences, near Kiltealy, was
famous for its sporting gatherings, and was
at the height ofits splendour and popularity
idle of the cighteenth century,

it transpires in the discussion that the policy
of the local papers is not above reproach.
The attitude of the Nationalist Press of
Cork towards the G.A.A. seems to have
suffered some modification of late. We
suspect the reason of this, but may not
state it, Whether it will be borne tamely
remains to be seen.

BheGaelic Athlete

Saturday, December 19th, 1914

Sq

EDITORIAL. \

‘Tue report of a discussion which took place
Cork County Board meeting last week,
in reference to the attitude of the Cork
Press towards the G.A.A,, is worthy of
careful perusal. It betokens a rather new
departure on che part of the Cork news-
papers, who, to give them their duc, have
teen by no means niggardy in the degree of
stention they formely bestowed upon the
National pestimes. While one’ of the
Cork papers bas gone out of its own way"
to adopt a new species of felon-setting

attack by placing the responsibility for the
failure of the recruiting campaign on the

Pundealgan Press for Irish Koll Ticketa,

regretted more than he could say that the
‘oceasion should have arisen for him to call
their attention to an-atticle which appeared
in one ofthe Cork papers on Saturday last
~The Curh Free Prat, . He would’ read

which ran as

the article in question,
follows :—
“We assert, humiliating though- the

confession is, that the pleasures of the
“hurling field and football field are more
answerable than the entire anti-enlist-
“ment press for the antipathy to the
recruiting. sergeant, which «the very
newspapers that Aagellate the footballers
of England for the same vice, have the
« jmpudence to trace to “ Trish Sedition.”

Continuing, the chairman said that they
had nothing (o say to recruiting, neither
for nor against it, a5 they were x purcly
sporting body. He thought that the County
Board should strongly express their disap
proval for such an unwaranted attack, an
attack, which was rendered all the
worse, as that Board had been paying
considerable amounts to the papers long
before the War had begun and in all
probability: would be long after.

Mr. D. King, (Collegians) said the chair-
had forestalled him on that occasion. He
had, for some time ‘past, been anxious (0
draw their attention to the attitude of the
press towards the Association, but owing to
‘more impartant matters being discussed, had
not a chance of doing so. He would not
however confine his remarks to one paper
but would include both. Notwithstanding
that the Board had been, and was, paying
considerable amounts to the papers for
advertising purposes, their affairs were
being slowly boycotted, and worse than
that, one of them had now thought it fit to
stab them in the back. It was worthy of
note that nothing had been said against the
foreign games players, but they there that
night were only Gaels and deserved no
consideration. Perhaps it was because the
Corporation and the County Council had
tried to blackguard them, the papers thought
they could do likewise, but he coul.
them, and all the other shoneens concerned,
they Were making the mistake of their lives,
‘The chairman then referred to the action of
the Government in commandeering the
only Gaelie grounds in the city and
he (the speaker) would ask those around
him : why wasn’t the Mardyke Rugby
grounds commandeered, for it was emin-
ently more suitable for the purpose than
thei grounds? The answer to that que.
tion was so obvious that it need not be
answered. Continuing he said this matter
fof the Press was very vital to their interests
and in fact the Association generally, and
drastic steps should be taken. He would,
therefore, move: “That the advertise:
ments of that Board be withdrawn from
the Cork Examiner, Cark Free Preis, and”
Brenrng Eche.”

‘The motion was not seconded, and the
discussion ended.

cessful career as an athlete, bis states-

absorbing Kindness which makes it

appear that it is you and not he that

is to be thanked when he confers
favours on you, bis utter lack of guile
in the wicked sense of the term, all
mark him as a man in a million. We
felt we could listen to him for days on

end as, be lifted some of the veils that
fogged’ Ir cs of the

days on end on the man and

Wexford County Board

OBJECTION TO KERRY WITHDRAWN

At special meeting of County Board on
th inst, the chairman, Mr. John Kennedy,
Said the meeting had been called to consider
whether ‘or not they should proceed with
the objection which theie secretary had
lodged to Kerry being awarded the All-
Ireland football final. ‘The circumstances of
the case rendered it absolutely necessary for
the secretary to lodge the objection. The
facts were that" the secretary received a
wire stating that Mr. Breen, the full for-
ward of the Kerry team, bad played in
London in this year's championships, and
he immediately lodged an objection pend-
ing, of course, the decision of the county
committee, and in order to safeguard his
‘own position, It was now for the county
committee to decide whether or not they
would proceed with the objection. Per-
sonally be was teetotally opposed to an
objection being proceeded with, whether
they had grounds or not for doing so. If
they on the objection it would mean that
Wexford would be champions and there
would be no replay, The result would
amount to this that Wexford would be
champions of a match on paper which they
failed to win on the field, He thought he
was voicing the opinions of every member
of the team when he said that Wexford did
not want an All-Ireland final under such
conditions. If it were a fact that Mr.
Breen did play in the London championships
and was, therefore, ineligible to play in the
All-Ireland final, he (chairman) considered
it very unfair of Kerry to play him if they
were aware of the fact.

The Secretary said he had not much to
add as his position had been fully explained
by the chaicman. It was late on Sacurday
evening when he received information about
the player in question, and as a precaution=
ary measure, he was'obliged to lodge the
objection at once, Personally he did not
advocate the lodging of objections in any
shape or form,

Mr. Kehoe—The era of objections has
gone, I think Wexford has set up a very
high standard of sportsmanship ; we won't

be the first to lower the standacd in any GAELS! When you bave a Printing

way. I quite concur with what the chair
man and secretary have stated, Tt would
be perfectly unthinkable to lodgy:an abject~
jon to sportsmen such as the Kerry inca in
general have proved themselves to be,

Te will be noticed that in the last line of
the third last verse of the song just quoted
that the goal is referred t0 as “the bow".
“© Guarding the bow ”” is very often used
finvold county Wexford hurling songs.as the
‘equivalent for the modern goal-keeper. The

“bow” of the old-time songs was much ‘poi

more casy to guard, anda much more
dificule mark for the forward player
‘than the up-to-date goal with its liberal
scoring space. The “bow” was formed
of two sallies bent in the form from which
it probably took itsname. Inthe Art and
Industrial exhibition, held in connection
with’the great county Feis at Wexford in
1913, some of the local Gaels brought me
